@michael_nielsen I agree that past scarcity can become the basis for present self-fulfilling prophecies and thus more scarcity in the future.
But this is one step away from the trauma narrative of "I am poor now because I was poor in the past and therefore I will always be poor"

I think the causality is different than that.
1. You believe you are dependent on others -->
2. You fear of being disliked by others (because they could take things away from you that you need) -->
3. In order to avoid rejection and dislike by others, you self-reject and self-dislike. "Insecurity". -->
4. You repress desires, boundaries, anger, etc.— anything that you fear other people will dislike. The goal is that you can't express them, and you achieve that by rejecting them and not being able to access them -->
5. You allow yourself to get into a bunch of situationships

Environmental allergies are curable? (Sublingual immunotherapy) https://t.co/tNNVdhNO5R

I used to have severe environmental allergies to cats, dogs, and the outdoors. As I kid, I woke up crying most mornings because of my allergies, and I would occasionally wake up with croup and difficulty breathing. I even had to be taken the hospital once.
Anyways, I cried enough that my mother found and enrolled me in a study for an experimental treatment called sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT).
For the next two years I took under-the-tongue drops, and I presume the drops were formulated with small amounts of the allergens I was reactive to.
My allergies have been nearly non-existent since then. Full post: https://t.co/hPX3RbZwxQ

I first learned about the concept of psychological boundaries a little over a year ago. But something didn’t seem quite right about it. People explained to me how they understood psychological boundaries, and but the explanations didn’t seem logically consistent. Sometimes,…

I first learned about the concept of psychological boundaries a little over a year ago. But something didn’t seem quite right about it. People explained to me how they understood psychological boundaries, and but the explanations didn’t seem logically consistent. Sometimes, boundaries meant ~“the unavoidable separateness of individuals”, and sometimes, boundaries meant ~“things you are physically capable of doing but other people might not *want* you to do”.

Since then, I’ve spent hundreds of hours and spoken to more than a hundred people thinking about this.

How I understand boundaries

Notice your arm. Notice that you have more access to your arm than anyone else: You have a far greater ability to control and feel it than anyone else. I can’t control or feel your arm nearly as well as you can, and you can’t control or feel my arm nearly as well as I can. 

More generally, I can’t (directly) control your actions, and you can’t (directly) control my actions.

Similarly, I can’t know what you are thinking or feeling, unless you somehow communicate this to me, and you can’t know what I am thinking or feeling, unless I somehow communicate this to you.

These are the natural boundaries that always exist between people: I can basically only control and feel parts of the universe that are me, and you can basically only control and feel parts of the universe that are you. 

I think many social conflicts are just misunderstandings of these boundaries. A few examples:

If you expect them to dislike you, they will.

If you unconsciously expect that someone will dislike you, they might pick up on that and on some level think, “Huh, maybe I should dislike this person.”

If you unconsciously expect that someone will distrust you, you might get…

If you expect them to dislike you, they will.

If you unconsciously expect that someone will dislike you, they might pick up on that and on some level think, “Huh, maybe I should dislike this person.”

If you unconsciously expect that someone will distrust you, you might get defensive and proactively try to show that they can trust you. But they might notice that and basically think, “Why are you trying to prove yourself? Are you not already worthy? Hm, maybe I *should* distrust you.”

More self-fulfilling prophecies:

Being Manipulated

If you unconsciously expect that other people can control and manipulate you, then you might not use your inalienable power to resist.

Negotiation Power

If you expect that you don’t have negotiation power to negotiate for what you want with others (e.g., salary, social power), you may not even try.

Sexual Rejection

If you unconsciously expect that sex is a bad thing or that they won’t want to have sex with you, they’ll probably pick up on that!

Not getting funding

See: self-fulfilling prophecies when applying for funding.

Newcomb’s Problem

If you expect that you would two-box, then that’s the setup you’ll receive.

Negative emotions

If you unconsciously expect that negative emotions are bad, then yeah anytime you notice a negative emotion you will conclude it’s bad.

---

My conjectures:
1/ Most people would get significantly better at friendship, romance, sex, agency, productivity, and communication if only they untangled their main emotional insecurities.
2/ It is possible to move from insecure attachment to secure attachment in a single evening with an excellent counselor.
3/ You can ‘solve’ many conflicts you have with other people just by untangling your own emotional insecurities. No need to involve the other party!
4/ Behaving and feeling unenlightened (e.g., experiencing the conscious and unconscious as very separate, having emotional insecurities, experiencing ego, etc.) is a useful strategy in many common social environments.
5/ Individual psychological growth may seem difficult and slow, but this is mostly just a side effect of individuals blocking efficient communication with their unconscious.
6/ It is best to assume that you’re already self-loving and “trying your best”. It is best to assume mistake theory before conflict theory for yourself and others.
7/ Many social phenomena are the result of self-fulfilling prophecies (ie: mental predictions that prove themselves). For example, if you expect that a particular person might dislike or distrust you, that might cause them to dislike/distrust you.
8/ “Morality” is irrelevant. All that matters is whether others cooperate with you.
9/ The experience of suffering is mainly the result of a mental self-fulfilling prophecy.

1/ Radically effective and rapid psychological growth is possible with the right combination of counselor and method. But this is rare in 2024!
2/ If you have a psychological issue like depression, anxiety, insecurity, failure at work or in romance, muscle tension, etc., and it…

1/ Radically effective and rapid psychological growth is possible with the right combination of counselor and method. But this is rare in 2024!
2/ If you have a psychological issue like depression, anxiety, insecurity, failure at work or in romance, muscle tension, etc., and it has persisted for a long time, then it can be extremely useful to view it as serving an adaptive purpose: as a strategy for avoiding fears or achieving goals. 
3/ Introspection is difficult for most people. The mind is mostly unconscious (AKA “in the body”). But introspection is doable with the right framework.
4/ The mind is mostly like millions of pattern predictors that run in parallel and do so automatically. For example one might be, “If tongue tastes bitter, then spit out food.”
5/ Psychological growth requires not just learning new predictions but also unlearning past predictions. 
6/ Psychological growth is largely about self-fulfilling prophecies. For example, expecting a job interview to go well can be self-fulfilling, and expecting a date to go poorly can also be self-fulfilling.
7/ The process of psychological growth is as unique as a fingerprint. Broadly similar, but still subtly different for each individual.
8/ For most people, psychological growth is mostly about improving their social interactions.
9/ This should be obvious, but some psychological growth can occur by improving one’s physical environment (e.g., reducing lead poisoning, eating better food, darkness at night for better sleep).

More on my blog.

I think it's not that "muscle tension arises when you resist a thought or emotion". I think it's that you create the muscle tension in order to avoid feeling the body-sensation of that thought/emotion.
I resolved my chronic (3.5y) neck pain last month, and afterwards it became obvious that much of my neck tension was because there was a feeling underneath that I didn't want to feel. Only just before I fixed it did I understand what the feeling was. For me, there was a feeling at the base of my neck that represented "the possibility that I could leave the social interaction I'm having right now". But for whatever reason, I learned that was not okay, and in order to suppress the possibility I (my nervous system) created muscle tension around the feeling so I could ignore it.
The trouble was that the feeling wanted to be felt, and so it would get accordingly stronger. And I would need to tense my neck even more to distract myself from the feeling. Feedback loop… Some days in the past few years I haven't been able to turn my head at all.
There were other feedback loops creating tension in my neck, too, but that was a large part of it.
Once I realized all of the feedback loops on a somatic level, and once I untangled my resistance to feeling that feeling and the other things, I learned to interrupt this feedback loop and I haven't had neck pain in 6 weeks since.
There's more, I'll write about this on my blog eventually

https://t.co/hiQKrUtjeK If I had to guess:
1) untangling a bunch of emotional insecurities. (unconscious predictions of the form, "In order for me to feel how I want, the world/other people must do things I want.") Muscle tension seems often associated with these, albeit for reasons i don't entirely understand yet.

2) self-massage. Not as a way to relax muscles -- I don't think massage has long-term effects all by itself. But as a way for me to get acquainted with my muscles, notice when and where I'm tense, and ultimately to become more connected with the part of my nervous system that is actually creating muscle tension. Then I release.

Yes, even self-loathing has a payoff. Let’s systematize how to overcome self-loathing. I used to think self-loathing was just bad. Then I realized it was trying to protect me.
I felt pretty bad about myself when I first realized that I was emotionally numb. “Why am I rejecting my feelings? That’s so bad!”

But ultimately, I noticed that my self-rejection was chasing a payoff.

By “payoff” I mean an unconscious benefit or protection that is predicted to come from the behavior — even if it’s not ideal:

Even my self-loathing was for a payoff!

I was just trying to stay safe! Once I saw this, I couldn’t dislike myself for that anymore.

(Something similar helped me accept— and ultimately undo— my depression.)

Many times I’ve guided clients of mine through the same process when they dislike themself. Here’s a sketch:

1. “What would you like instead?” Self-love. (Identifying the desired state)
2. “How does self-love feel when you do have it? Where in your body?” (Connecting with the desired state)
3. “OK. Now ask that feeling, What bad thing happens if we have this in all situations? Tell me what associations come into your awareness.” (Uncovering unconscious fears)
4. I pay attention to what they say, and ask incisive questions to bring their attention to potential unconscious confusions. (Socratic dialogue)

Most commonly, the response they get from their unconscious parts is like,

> If I love myself, then other people will get mad at me.

So self-loathing becomes a strategy: If you dislike yourself first, you'll do less, interact less, and fewer people will get mad at you.

But I don’t tell my clients this. All I do is ask them Socratic questions. Surprisingly, everyone I’ve done this with so far has realized that self-loathing was just an attempt to stay safe.

Even their self-loathing comes from self-love! How else could it be?

What I want more to understand about the unconscious is that it's not one thing, but a billion. Billions (maybe trillions) of pattern predictors operate in parallel. If one part of "your unconscious" is doing X… another part could be doing something entirely different

Which also means that if you have some unconscious prediction creating anxiety/insecurity/unwanted behavior, before you can get it to update, you must first find it. Many attempts fail to take this into account, and it's why willpower often fails

Visa: but, in general, **tell the scroller how you want them to engage with your content**. What is the desired response? Think more user-centric/reader-centric.

Each had a conflict with me, so with each:
1. I heard them out until when I ask “Anything more?” they say No.
2. I asked what they want from me, or what they want to hear anything from me. Specific questions.
3. I did NOT say anything except in response to concrete questions from them. I encouraged them to ask questions instead. (It never works when I defend myself and answer questions they didn’t ask!)
4. I shared how I feel
5. Some crying
6. shared feedback loops that seem to be going on - eg if you react poorly whenever I tell you things of course I’m not going to tell you things. They never realized. Other feedback loops like that.
7. “Anything else you want to air? Anything else you want from me?” “No.”
8. Things I want them to do, eg ask questions if you don’t understand me and please not jump to conclusions and assume-by-default that I’m a bad son.

But now I think we’ll be able to talk about conflicts as they come up so hopefully conflicts won’t queue up like this.

Time will tell.

Have you ever felt deeply connected to the world around you? Like your effortless existence makes a positive difference?

I’ve been pondering this state lately, especially since re-reading The Courage to be Disliked. The book introduces a concept I like called “Community… https://t.co/IkuRMzGnmy

Have you ever felt deeply connected to the world around you? Like your effortless existence makes a positive difference?

I’ve been pondering this state lately, especially since re-reading The Courage to be Disliked. The book introduces a concept I like called “Community Feeling”.

Community Feeling is fully believing “I am beneficial to the world” and “my effortless existence contributes positively to the community”. Your community could include other people, and also animals, plants, rocks, the universe, and even everything across all time.

Community Feeling is feeling part of a greater whole.
People who have Community Feeling feel connected to others and have a strong sense of self-worth. Who doesn’t want that?

How do I know if I’m contributing?
You might think, “I’ll know I’m contributing positively when others tell me I am.” But how do you know they’re correct? How do you actually know if you’re contributing positively?

I’m not sure we can.

I used to constantly seek external validation, thinking it would confirm my positive impact. Instead, it left me feeling uncertain and dependent on others' opinions.

Now, I do something different: I have faith. I’m trying my best to do good and be competent, and I trust that good intentions and attempted competence will, on average, lead to better outcomes.

I’m also working on untangling my barriers to feeling Community Feeling.

Common barriers to Community Feeling:
Believing others are threats
The more you believe others are threats, the more trouble you will have feeling Community Feeling.
- How comfortable do you feel maintaining your physical autonomy in social situations?
- Are you able to maintain your emotional autonomy when interacting with others?

Personally, after I became more secure, I became much less afraid of cooperating closely with others.

For example, I used to avoid many social situations because I feared being “controlled” by others’ opinions or expectations. But as I worked on untangling my emotional insecurities, I found that I could maintain my autonomy while still engaging deeply with others.

Doubting others’ efforts or intentions
The more you doubt others’ efforts or intentions, the more you hesitant you will be to form a super-agent with them.
- Do you often assume people are acting out of self-interest rather than genuine care for the community?
- Have you ever misjudged someone’s intentions and later realized they were trying to help?

Personally, I used to often be skeptical that some others’ efforts were good faith attempts to do good things for the world. Essentially, I believed that they were “evil”. But these days, I believe pretty strongly that everyone is trying their best for their ideal of good. Even criminals, etc. I may not agree with their definition of “good”, but I don’t doubt that they have one. 

I’ve seen many people have trouble with this though. They seem to believe that having faith in someone means endorsing their behavior, but it doesn’t.

Doubting the value of others
The more you doubt the value of others, the less you will want to identify with them.
- How often do you find yourself doubting the worth or potential of those around you?
- Can you recall a time when someone surprised you with their capabilities or kindness?

Personally, I often find myself skeptical of others’ competence (e.g. for others who write about similar topics to this blog). I do not feel Community Feeling when I am in this state! But I’m not yet sure what my resistance is, so I probably have unconscious predictions to untangle here. But even if I don’t appreciate their results I can still appreciate their intentions to work on a problem I agree is important.

Where in your life would you like to feel more connected to others?

The old incentive structure for my growth service was simple: declare a bounty, achieve your goal, pay when ready. Results-based payment allows me to collect reliable efficacy data while also pushing me to help resolve issues as efficiently as possible, sometimes in a single…

The old incentive structure for my growth service was simple: declare a bounty, achieve your goal, pay when ready. Results-based payment allows me to collect reliable efficacy data while also pushing me to help resolve issues as efficiently as possible, sometimes in a single session. As demand grew, I increased the minimum from $3k to $15k to stay at equilibrium.

The trouble was that growth follows different paths for different people. Some integrate insights quickly, others benefit from sustained practice and support. The old structure didn't accommodate this range well.

The new structure:
1. Client defines their goals and a bounty: “If there was a magic button that could resolve your biggest blocks, how much would you happily pay to press it?”
2. We check for fit
3. (New) We work intensively for about a month (typically 2-3 sessions, 6-9 total hours, plus texting support); a refundable deposit may be required
4. (New) If ongoing support is necessary, we negotiate an additional hourly rate
5. They pay their bounty whenever they feel satisfied

The new incentive structure is designed such that:
• They achieve their goals as efficiently as possible
• They receive ongoing support when needed
• I collect reliable efficacy data
• I know they’re committed

Locally optimal psychology

There’s no way that chronic depression, self-loathing, poor agency, or muscle tension could be *optimal*… right? 

Jake was depressed for 6 months. He also felt horrible every time he interacted with other people because of his emotional insecurities.… https://t.co/obCQZKLN6j

Locally optimal psychology

There’s no way that chronic depression, self-loathing, poor agency, or muscle tension could be *optimal*… right? 

Jake was depressed for 6 months. He also felt horrible every time he interacted with other people because of his emotional insecurities.

So without knowing how to outgrow his insecurities, his system basically had two options:

1. Interact with other people — and constantly feel horrible
2. Don’t interact with anyone unless absolutely necessary

So his system converged on the second option, also known as “depression”. 

Depression certainly wasn’t the globally optimal strategy, but given the options, it was a locally optimal strategy.

[🔴Depression, the best strategy known and accessible at the time.]

Once he outgrew his emotional insecurities, however, he was no longer blocked on the better optimum of both interacting with others and not feeling horrible.

And so with no need for the ‘depression strategy’, the symptoms evaporated — two years and counting. I know because Jake was me.

More examples of locally optimal strategies

Most chronic issues for the people I help end up looking like locally optimal strategies. For example, self-loathing often turns out to be a strategy for avoiding conflict with others. Lack of agency often turns out to be a strategy for avoiding judgements of failure. But ideally, they would both have self-love and be safe from conflicts; or have agency and be okay with judgements of failure.

I’ve seen people make significant and sometimes total progress in weeks on issues they’ve had for years. One of my tenets is that any persistent mental issue is probably a locally optimal strategy. (Again: if my mind had hit the “undo depression” button while I was depressed, I would’ve gotten hurt!)

In my own growth, my issues relating to depression, empathy, conflict avoidance, emotional numbness, eye contact, boundaries, neck pain, and more all turned out to be locally optimal strategies. Only once I fully understood what an issue was doing for me did I make a step change towards resolving it.

For example, I had neck pain for 3½ years. A few times it was so bad I couldn’t turn my head. Over the years, I had tried to counteract my neck tension with physical therapy and stretching but nothing really worked. Then, earlier this year I finally realized precisely how it was strategic, so I implemented better strategies towards the same goals and have had ~90% less neck pain since.

Btw: Noticing how my neck pain was locally optimal was quite tricky, and even suppressed. So even if an issue IS a locally optimal strategy, it can be quite difficult to understand how. 

How common are locally optimal strategies?

I have no hard data, but I suspect that when an issue has lasted years, local optimality is more probable than not. Why? Consider:

If there were no downsides to resolving a persistent issue, then why has it lasted so long??

Just one more exposure bro

“Just expose yourself to more social situations!” — Ah yes, you felt anxious the first 100 times, but the 101st will be the breakthrough!

“But exposure works!” people yell from across the street. “Like for fear of snakes - you know, those things you see once a year!”

Uh, it’s pretty rational to fear things you have little experience with. But social anxiety… you interact with people everyday! Why would anything change after the first 100 attempts?

I don’t doubt that a couple of exposures can often reduce anxieties. However, if you still feel anxious even after hundreds of social situations and years of trying... then maybe your fear is actually doing something presently useful and you should reconnect with your intuitions.

At a 100% eye contact workshop I led earlier this year, most people became comfortable quickly with essentially a guided meditation. 

But one guy was still struggling. I had him tune into his feelings and ask them: “What bad thing happens if I feel good about eye contact?”

To his own surprise, “heartbreak” was the word that came out.

He felt it out: "We make eye contact… we fall in love… we break up."

This guy could’ve easily spent years forcing himself to make eye contact without discovering that he needed to make heartbreak safe. Had he just followed the standard advice — "Just expose yourself more!" — he likely would’ve gotten hurt!

So once he found a way to make eye contact with heartbreak being safe, for the rest of the night he effortlessly maintained continuous and unbroken eye contact just like every other attendee of the workshop.

This is what I mean when I say emotional issues are often locally optimal strategies — they're often serving a kinda reasonable purpose, even if you don’t “know” it. 

Another person I helped had dealt with social anxiety his entire life. Before talking to me, he had tried “1 year of actively seeking exposure to scary situations” and “8 years of talk therapy”.

Instead of more exposure, in a long conversation I helped him locate and reconnect with the strategies of his anxiety (in this case it seemed to be unconscious predictions that if others disliked him, it would be bad). Once he learned to feel okay regardless of others’ reactions — which he determined was safe in his life circumstances (which isn’t the case for everyone tbc) — then he didn't need the “social anxiety strategy” anymore.

Over the next month, he practiced this new way of relating to his social anxiety. When difficult situations arose, he would tune into both the anxiety and the feeling of unconditional okayness:

> I had a discussion with another co-worker where he made me feel quite stupid and I left kind of dreading our next interaction. After noticing this I used the technique I had learned of holding both my embarrassment/dread and the emotion I wanted to feel instead, excitement, together 'in my body.' I rid myself of the dread in a couple minutes, and came back the next day eager to talk.

Another example:

This is the kind of “exposure” that I endorse: trying on the feelings you want in everyday life to discover where you’re blocked. But again, it only worked because he was intentional about it! “The technique I had learned of holding both my embarrassment/dread and the emotion I wanted to feel instead, excitement, together 'in my body.'”

A month after we spoke he sent this update:

> I'm actually feeling excited about entering social situations where the chances of things going worse than I would want are high. 

He also sent $3,000 as thanks, and hasn’t asked for another (free) session since. We plan to record a 6-month reflection podcast soon (will be posted on twitter and on my blog).

I'm pretty sure that more exposure alone wouldn’t have helped him get better.

A better way to do matchmaking: by example
When friends ask me to matchmake them, they usually list traits they want: “Kind, curious, growth-minded...” But I find it really hard to think of the right people from these descriptions.

Here's what works better: "You know [ex's name]? Do you know anyone kinda like them?"

Matching by description is hard. When someone lists desired traits, I need to:
- Understand what they mean by each trait
- Think of people I know
- Check if they match
- Somehow weigh everything together

But when I meet their ex, I instantly get it - their vibe, how they talk, their humor, how they move through the world.

Plus, there's usually a gap between what people think they want and what works for them. Their trait list comes from trying to rationalize past attractions and guess future ones. But seeing who they've actually had chemistry with shows me what actually works.

An example also captures subtle compatibility factors they might not even be aware of. Maybe they say they want someone "growth-minded," but what they really click with is a specific style of playful intellectual banter that's hard to describe but easy to spot.

some meditation advice has a vibe like... “to become more present, all you need to do is practice! it's just a skill, like learning to ride a bike 😊”

this never worked for me. when I tried to force presence through practice, i made little progress.

being present isn't a skill to build — it's the natural state! What blocks presence is unconscious predictions of bad outcomes. Remove these blocks to make presence automatic.

1. I struggled to be present with emotions in my body. Daily meditation didn't help. Finally I asked: “What bad thing happens if I feel my feelings?” My system responded: emotional pain would be overwhelming, feelings would make me less productive, expressing emotions would anger others. After untangling these predictions, emotional presence became much easier.

2. With others' emotions, I felt I'd be obligated to fix their pain if I truly experienced it. After addressing these predictions, empathetic presence improved significantly. 

3. When investigating my chronic neck tension, instead of trying to "be more present" with the pain, I looked for what bad things I felt would happen if I fully felt it. My system revealed: feeling the sensation meant acknowledging I could leave social situations that felt unsafe, which itself felt dangerous. After untangling this and other blocks, most of my tension resolved. I'm still finding and untangling more blocks. 

(Reply if you have any Qs about these examples)

Now, you might wonder: if presence isn't a skill, why do some people seem to benefit from meditation practice? In my experience, meditation can help with noticing blocks and creating space to work with them. But the practice itself isn't building presence so much as creating opportunities to identify and release blocks that prevent presence. This is what I believe explains why some people can meditate for years without becoming much more present, and others grow quickly once they find and integrate their core blocks.

Most of what I’ve done to develop more presence is ask my system: “What bad thing happens if I'm fully present right now?”

One last thing: Once you become fully present, you may be tempted to think “Oh, I get it now! Being present is so simple! Just be present!” But that will overlook all the little blocks you untangled along the way.

Natural presence is already there, waiting to be uncovered.

Why boundaries are essential: they create autonomy

Imagine a bacterium:
It has insides: its guts. 

It has outsides: its environment.

And in between, a membrane. 

The membrane is what attempts to control what comes in or goes out. For example, pores can guide nutrients and waste.
The membrane is what maintains the bacterium’s separation from the environment.

Boundaries can grant autonomy.
For a bacterium to stay autonomous, its membrane must not dissolve.

In general, for an agent to stay more autonomous, its boundaries must not dissolve. 

(A bacterium is just one kind of agent, and “boundaries” are just another term for “membrane”.)

Suitable boundaries grant more autonomy.

Boundaries can provide safety without physical distance.
Let’s say an agent (blue) is endangered by a threat (red).

What could make blue safe?

One way is physical distance:

(Typically it’s harder for something to hurt you/cause effects on you if it’s far away.)

Another way is suitable boundaries:
Boundaries can provide “causal distance”.
In this way, suitable boundaries provide causal distance without physical distance. 

Boundaries can allow cooperation.
When agents can rely on boundaries for safety (as opposed to relying on physical distance), they can interact more closely with each other. 

And when agents interact more closely, they can cooperate more!

To preserve autonomy, preserve boundaries.

Based on Alex Hormozi's book, here are several key insights you could apply to improve your bounty service:

Value Equation Enhancement:

Increase perceived likelihood of achievement by showcasing clear testimonials and concrete examples of your clients' successes
Decrease time delay by showing how quickly people can start seeing results with your systems
Decrease perceived effort & sacrifice by making your process as seamless as possible
Focus on the dream outcome (growth) your clients want to achieve

Offer Structure Improvements:

Break down your service into component parts rather than presenting it as one big offering
Stack multiple valuable elements into compelling bundles
Add supporting tools/resources that make implementation easier
Consider including done-for-you elements to reduce client effort

Pricing Strategy:

Position as premium pricing rather than competing on cost
Charge based on the value delivered, not time spent
Consider performance-based pricing models where you share in the upside
Use price anchoring by comparing to alternatives or breaking down ROI

Risk Reversal:

Offer strong guarantees tied to client actions/implementation
Consider service-based guarantees where you keep working until they achieve results
Use implied guarantees through performance-based compensation
Make the guarantee specific and measurable

Differentiation:

Create a "category of one" where you can't be directly compared to alternatives
Focus on specific niches/avatars rather than trying to serve everyone
Use unique naming and positioning to stand out
Bundle services in ways competitors don't

Market Selection:

Target markets with high pain, purchasing power, and ease of targeting
Focus on growing markets rather than shrinking ones
Consider niching down further to command higher prices
Look for markets where your solution provides clear ROI

Enhancers:

Add scarcity through limited spots/cohorts
Create urgency through deadline-driven promotions
Stack valuable bonuses that enhance the core offering
Use seasonal promotions and varied "wrappers" to maintain freshness

Operational Excellence:

Start with over-delivery to prove the model
Systematize and streamline over time
Focus on client results and testimonials
Build processes that scale

The key takeaway is to focus on creating an offer so good that prospects "feel stupid saying no" by maximizing perceived value while minimizing risk and effort required. This requires careful attention to how you package, position, and deliver your services.
Consider implementing these elements gradually, testing what resonates most with your market. The goal is to transform your service from a commodity into a unique, high-value offering that commands premium prices while delivering exceptional results for clients.

just ran a computationally efficient matchmaking event: 1) personality-polarizing questions, 2) go to the corner that best represents your answer, 3) chat with those who answered similarly.  
we verified the core mechanism for how we might run a 1,000-person matching event
- overall went pretty well with no major problems!
- questions were great. eg “are you flexible enough to place your palms on the ground” or “place yourself on the quadrants cat-dog/brat-demure”. we only thought of these today so it's prob easy to do much better
- it worked better as a friendship-making event than romantic. this is better vibes anyway
- everyone (everyone who stayed) reported having a moderate level of fun. lots hung around after until the venue closed
- friends were made!

How do people intentionally stress-test their romantic relationships?
- “went on vacation together”
- “went to burning man together”
- “Went to IKEA together” (!!)
 - “One of my favorite first dates was taking the LSAT as a competition”
- played with kids together
- introduced them to friends + family asap. 1) do you feel embarrassed to do so? 2) do your friends and family notice anything off about them?
- “fix something complicated together (eg look for health insurance)”
- “get lost together, no google maps for a day”
- play games where there's a winner and loser - is there poor sportsmanship?

What can we do to help others “break up faster”? (tell asap when your relationship is doomed)
- Run an IKEA event for couples !! ??
- run an event with models of good relationships ? invite longstanding healthy relationships + also new relationships. people benefit by seeing what healthy relationships look like?
- “relationship fortune-telling”, coach who predicts if your relationship will work out. (John Gottmann but better)
- “36 questions to know if you need to break up”?

lmk if you want to help make any of these happen!

If a cavity has reached dentin, their role is much more limited – they cannot magically rebuild the dentin on their own. So far, no peer-reviewed clinical trial has shown complete dentin regeneration from these peptides in an advanced carious lesion. They work on early enamel, and potentially as adjuncts for dentin surface remineralization or pulp capping, but a deeper cavity still typically requires conventional treatment (drilling and filling) or emerging regenerative procedures.

most of my clients start with a tiny misconception that can wreck their progress: https://t.co/UZzQpzSk8z

they expect progress to feel like getting over a cold: each day the discomfort eases a little more. 

sure, there may be some dips, but it never snaps back to first-day intensity. you mostly just keep improving until, one morning, you barely remember being sick.

not only is this picture wrong, but it can set back your growth. when you believe progress is a climb upward, then a rough day or rough week feels like proof that your efforts haven’t helped at all.

This dentist applied a new treatment to regenerate my cavities with natural enamel.

I finally found a sane dentist!

Recap:
Don't you hate it when your dentist prescribes fillings for cavities you don't actually have? My previous dentist said I had 7 cavities, all need fillings… https://t.co/txV9GGDpfC

This dentist applied a new treatment to regenerate my cavities with natural enamel.

I finally found a sane dentist!

Recap:
Don't you hate it when your dentist prescribes fillings for cavities you don't actually have? My previous dentist said I had 7 cavities, all need fillings asap, pay $3k. But I was like, hold on, made the tweet below, did research with o3, discovered a regenerative dental medicine called Curodont, and looked for new dentists. 

In the end, I found probably the best dentist in San Francisco (for my epistemics at least), discovered 4 of the “cavities” had already healed, had the remaining 3 treated regeneratively, and walked away with no permanent teeth damage!

What's Curodont?
A new dental medicine that can regenerate early cavities with natural enamel.

Despite this tech being insanely cool, few dentists in the US offer it yet. So I found a dentist in SF who did. I didn't know if Curodont would fit my particular case, but surely any dentist who used it (and mentioned it on their website) would be sane.

What dentist did you find?
Dr. Keeper (San Francisco) (Turns out he had helped commercialize Curodont in the US actually)

My appointment:
- Got an infrared scan, Dr. Keeper determines 4 of the ““7 cavities”” were… already healed. Yes, there are spots on the X-rays my previous dentist took, but the enamel had already healed over those spots. There was no need for drilling.
- For the remaining 3 cavities, he applied Curodont to help regenerate the enamel. 
- We did some cool stuff with measuring the pH of my saliva. I'm low-risk for developing new cavities. My diet is great.
- (Other non-invasive treatments were available if my situation needed, but it didn't.)

I like Dr. Keeper a lot:
- He is the most respectful dentist I've ever had. With my previous dentist (ironically down the hall and named “soothing dental”), everything was a catrastrophe and they were super passive aggressive.
- HE IS SANE HE IS SANE HE IS REASONABLE. He more reasonable than any dentist I ever expected to meet.
- I told him about @LuminaProbiotic and he got really excited and asked a lot of questions. He hadn't heard of it, but knew about the older trial for the caries vaccine. He proactively brainstormed ways to help it stick to my teeth!! I connected him to the CEO, I hope he joins the cause.
- He is very interested in nanohydroxyapatite toothpaste and polish (hydroxyapatite is the mineral that compses teeth!). As I said, he is sane (unlike almost every other dentist in 2025, imo). I actually knew a little bit more about this than him on this point so he asked me to send him links to the OG brand I import from Japan (Sangi/Apaguard).
- (Again: He is reasonable! Every other dentist has shrugged me off when I bring up Lumina and nanohydroxyapatite. It seems this guy actually cares about improving his ability to help!)

That was it! I'm fine! I happily paid a few hundred dollars (I don't have dental insurance btw). No permanent mouth damage! I'm SO HAPPY dentists like him exist!!

How to (maybe) find a dentist like this in your city:
Under the assumption that any dentist who mentions Curodont on their website in 2025 is probably sane, you could try googling the string below (including the quote characters) to find such dentists:
"curodont" "<your city>"

---

Finally, the bounty prizes from the last tweet:
It appears I didn't have half of cavities to begin with, and it wasn't possible to tell if my cavities healed in specifically the time since the tweet. Nonetheless I'm happy to pay for the advice I was convinced of and used:
- $75 to @adic_9 for getting me to use xylitol, among other cool suggestions and effort with peptides
- $75 to @jakozloski for reminding me to take K2
- $40 to @zencephalon for suggesting SDF (I didn't use it, but o3 later convinced me it's a lot safer than i thought it was so I should've had it on my radar sooner, thanks)
- $(bounty refused) to @melissa for getting me to check my bones, eat more bones, and generally take health a lot more seriously. To pay it forward, I will reimburse 3 people reading this who are in high risk categories for low bone density (white/asian, small body frame, etc.) up to $50 for a DEXA scan. (If you live in SF, get a scan at Bodyspec.) Anyone reading this can DM me. 
- $(priceless) to @tzhongg for connecting me to @melissa

1. Feedback loops 🔁: I couldn't notice when I had learned helplessness or was manifesting self-fulfilling prophecies. Easier for someone else to point these out
2. Working memory constraints: ~Half of WM was used to do the coaching, leaving only half to receive it. This makes some complex emotional updates impossible to do alone.

I gained the ability to coach myself later only after working on related theories for 1-2y full-time and untangling a significant chunk with skilled help. But even now I don't see some insecurities until someone points at them. I often don't notice I'm suffering or tense or being suboptimal until someone points it out!

People generally don’t follow logical statements

Example below, I learned to refrain from writing phrases like “only if” (which require sequential logical processing)
And instead use phrases like “forbidden until” (which use parallel vibe-based processing)

People read “…forbidden…” → feel *forbidden vibe*
People read “…only if…” → uh oh, now they have to do logical series computation (and most people won’t because they’re reading quickly)

Vibe-based computation is faster because it runs in parallel. Logical computation is slower because it runs in series.

Another example: 
– "Free shipping on orders over $50"
vs
– "Shipping costs only apply to orders under $50"
Don't they feel different?

Emotional work improved my reaction time

Before emotional work: Dog runs after bunny, I hesitate ~2 sec before yelling, he’s gone

After emotional work: Dog runs after bunny, I yell “ATLAS!” before he lands his first leap, he stops in his tracks

~ 2 sec → ~ 0.5 sec!

Suprised… https://t.co/2FWPbGPnnW

Emotional work improved my reaction time

Before emotional work: Dog runs after bunny, I hesitate ~2 sec before yelling, he’s gone

After emotional work: Dog runs after bunny, I yell “ATLAS!” before he lands his first leap, he stops in his tracks

~ 2 sec → ~ 0.5 sec!

Suprised me - When I looked back to see what the difference was, I realized I used to have a ~subconscious internal debate: “Should I yell at him? Would that be bad? Is it ok to yell ta him? Will he be mad at me if I do?” And this was literally slowing down my reaction time!

Perception → thhhhhooouuuggghhhtttssss → Action

Then when I became less insecure, I stopped worrying (→ “I should just immediately tell others how I’m feeling, duh.”), the internal debate stopped happening, and my reaction time improved.
This shows the difference between learning (adding more techniques, thoughts, process, structure) and unlearning (removing) https://t.co/Kzy0JN2sB4

Yeah two things going on here:
1. Releases as something you feel. Emotional release. “Feeling a shift.”
2. Releases as the cause lasting growth. Insight. Memory reconsolidations.

Usually 1 precedes 2, however lots of things that look like 1 don't result in 2 and you know by waiting: https://t.co/2a5scdJknO

It's definitely possible to Goodhart on 1. Some coaches seem to maximize how often people cry, for example. But more 1 doesn't necessarily mean more 2. 

I can often predict when 1 will lead 2 due to local optimality etc etc, but that's not something that I know how to teach. So my advice is to track results and wait

So I rewrote The Courage to be Disliked so it's actually useful. Thread 1/ https://t.co/JgNFONJpwx

In the first part of this thread, I'm going to evaluate the core claims of the book. Then I'm going to rewrite them to be more effective.

Let's start with the claims:

Claim #1: Emotional “issues” often have hidden functions

TCTBD introduces an idea called teleology. It's a framework for understanding the logic behind your emotions by evaluating their use in the present https://t.co/5CidhkDplV

While teleology is the most important concept introduced in TCTBD, it’s explained extremely poorly!

Worse, the explanation needlessly triggers many of the people who’d benefit from the mindset.

In part 2 of this thread, I'll give my own explanation for this framework that's… https://t.co/UtTZZ6MgvD

While teleology is the most important concept introduced in TCTBD, it’s explained extremely poorly!

Worse, the explanation needlessly triggers many of the people who’d benefit from the mindset.

In part 2 of this thread, I'll give my own explanation for this framework that's more accessible

Claim #2: All problems are interpersonal relationship problems

While I don't believe ALL relationship problems are interpersonal, many (if not most) problems seem downstream of disliking yourself. Ie: the most common strategy to avoid interpersonal conflict https://t.co/TYqNugRiNH https://t.co/bNrLkx6lLZ

This is the idea behind the most popular post on the Locally Optimal blog

Essentially, most people can't stop disliking themself until they feel safe enough to be disliked by others.

My own research has proven this to be true over and over https://t.co/F7eDR4hKYT

Claim #3: Whether you outgrow your issues is your choice

Growth follows from the belief in growth. And if you believe you can't grow, you probably won't. https://t.co/FsWMTsgo6a

The problem with this is the authors don't acknowledge that learned helplessness is often teleological!!

Many people feel like outgrowing their issues is a subtly bad thing. Your “choice” to not grow could be serving a function!

Claim #4: You are autonomous, and so are other adults (Separation of tasks)

In other words, you can only control your own behavior and read your own mind, not anyone else’s. https://t.co/dNwkipJ2j4

While this seems straightforward, the book’s description of how to separate tasks is so muddled that I entirely missed it the first 4x I read the book!

Their explanation is also difficult to apply to real messy relationships, especially when strong emotions are involved. https://t.co/kLO70IpUMI

Claim #5: Separating tasks will improve your relationships

The book claims separating tasks “is enough to change one’s interpersonal relationships dramatically.” I actually think this is both very wrong and misleading as it reverses causality. https://t.co/ZJjcvwXxkD

What the book fails to mention is that your intellectual understanding of “separation of tasks” is powerless against strong feelings of insecurity.

You need to feel secure before you can separate tasks. You can’t just choose to separate tasks. https://t.co/DN6HUvDhv1

Claim #6: People need to feel like part of a greater whole

The most important takeaway from this section of the book is that humans thrive when they believe “my effortless existence benefits the group.”

But you have to take this belief on faith. https://t.co/9bs2MTIOqB

Claim #7: You can choose to be present

I think here the book is making the same mistake it did with separation of tasks.

It’s claiming you can choose to be present, when in reality you probably can’t https://t.co/kgN4QwQHw2

Now with that out of the way...

Part 2: How I’d rewrite The Courage to be Disliked

Improvement #1: A less triggering explanation of teleology

Drop the term "teleology." Stop focusing on what doesn't exist (ie "Trauma doesn't exist").

Focus on what does exist: emotional issues with hidden functions.

https://t.co/mWtHrdXNYt https://t.co/fI6H4KLIAZ

Improvement #3: Present better skill trees

This book implies that you should work on Level N skills (separating tasks, being present) before you have Level N-1 skills (having the courage to be disliked, feeling secure).

If I rewrote the book, I’d flesh out a better skill tree… https://t.co/JldcIIXAcz

Improvement #3: Present better skill trees

This book implies that you should work on Level N skills (separating tasks, being present) before you have Level N-1 skills (having the courage to be disliked, feeling secure).

If I rewrote the book, I’d flesh out a better skill tree for how to follow advice for different stages of the path.

Improvement #4: Validate the rewrite on real people!

Self-help gurus rarely seem to care whether their content helps the people with the issues they aim to address.

If I rewrote The Courage to be Disliked, I would test to see if the instructions had their intended effect. https://t.co/DSXE7kGikH

In conclusion, someone should rewrite TCTBD to make it more accessible.

For those interested in attempting this, my complete thoughts on TCTBD, a link to the full text of the book, and my contact info can be found here: https://t.co/WERYQf5sR1

I learned the hard way that my subconscious is smarter than my conscious. Back when I was lost, I resisted my intuitions and did approximately nothing for months. Eventually, I gave up. I let myself do whimsy, in this case writing for hours everyday. I had no clue how to make a living doing this, and I didn't have much savings, but so be it!-- Life felt effortless when I trusted my intuitions and tense when I doubted them. 

I developed my craft, met the right people, made a whole lot of mistakes, and continued to do *whatever felt interesting the moment*. Along the way I dramatically improved my wellbeing, created a new job, found ikigai, made great friends, and even set up the conditions for my amazing partner to find me. 

Sometimes I consider the alternate timeline where I had stuck with physics or interp. The timeline where I had doubted my intuitions and-- instead of writing for my silly blog with 200 subscribers-- had made the “rational” choice. I'm glad I chose this path.

“Emotional Blindness” is when you go blank or intellectualize when asked how you feel. You’re disconnected from subtle somatic signals that carry crucial emotional information: a tight chest, hollow stomach, or clenched jaw. Without this felt sense, you’re navigating in the dark.

Unlearning your anxiety is significantly easier when bottlenecks are targeted in order. Risks doing it out of order:

• Flaky breakthroughs: When earlier bottlenecks go unaddressed, “breakthroughs” tend to be flaky
• Learned helplessness: Experience enough flaky breakthroughs and you may come to feel that lasting growth is impossible.
• Overwhelm: A common experience when attempting deep inner work while experiencing crisis.
• More anxiety: If you increase your emotional awareness (#6) without being ready to do inner work (#1–4), you’ll just feel your existing anxiety more intensely without being able to resolve it. Not fun
• Loops of deep shame: If you attempt deep inner work (#6-8) without scaffolding (#5), it’s common to enter unhelpful feedback loops like endless rumination.
• More triggers: If you work on triggers (#8) before self-rejection (#7), your anxiety may shape-shift: public speaking fear becomes email fear becomes… *endless morphing*.

REBUS and the Anarchic Brain: Toward a Unified Model of the Brain Action of Psychedelics

R L Carhart-Harris, K J Friston

This paper formulates the action of psychedelics by integrating the free-energy principle and entropic brain hypothesis. We call this formulation relaxed beliefs under psychedelics (REBUS) and the anarchic brain, founded on the principle that—via their entropic effect on spontaneous cortical activity—psychedelics work to relax the precision of high-level priors or beliefs, thereby liberating bottom-up information flow, particularly via intrinsic sources such as the limbic system. We assemble evidence for this model and show how it can explain a broad range of phenomena associated with the psychedelic experience. With regard to their potential therapeutic use, we propose that psychedelics work to relax the precision weighting of pathologically overweighted priors underpinning various expressions of mental illness. We propose that this process entails an increased sensitization of high-level priors to bottom-up signaling (stemming from intrinsic sources), and that this heightened sensitivity enables the potential revision and deweighting of overweighted priors. We end by discussing further implications of the model, such as that psychedelics can bring about the revision of other heavily weighted high-level priors, not directly related to mental health, such as those underlying partisan and/or overly-confident political, religious, and/or philosophical perspectives.

Emotions are like loss signals pointing to an internal prediction error between your model of the world and reality.
When you fully feel the emotion — i.e., let the error propagate through the entire network without interruption — you perform complete backpropagation.
The gradient gets applied, weights are updated, and the loss goes to zero.
If you suppress or avoid the emotion, you clip the gradient: the model can’t converge, so the same error resurfaces later.

Emotions like loss signals, shows prediction error between your model of the world and the world.

Emotions like loss signals, shows prediction error between your model of the world and the world.
When you fully feel an emotion (let the error propagate through the entire network without interruption), complete backpropagation occurs. The gradient gets applied, weights are updated, and loss→0. 
Once a feeling is completely felt, you stop feeling it.
But when emotions are suppressed or avoided, the gradient gets clipped: the model can’t converge, so the same error can resurface later. So you may ruminate about past mistakes because you haven't completely updated yet.

Finally figured out exactly what's creepy about pickup artists.

You know when you start dating someone and they're rushing everything? Like they're trying to speedrun the relationship?

It's that they're playing a finite game inside of what you THOUGHT was an infinite game. They have a pre-set goal: “sex by date 3.” But you thought you both were playing “maybe we date forever, who knows, let’s see where this goes.”

The dissonance is creepiness.

Similar thing happens in friendships and business. Someone exploiting when you thought you both were exploring.

You feel it even if you can't name it.
